chapters.title: A good story is the most effective way to reach people with a message
chapters.text:
There is no room for uncertainty in a brand message. Aesthetics matter less than effective language. You may have the most beautiful website on the block, but if your language is unclear, that elegance is wasted. Your message should pass along three things: who you are, what you do, and why you are the best fit for the job. Customers should grasp your message and connect with your story without strain. If they keep running into friction, they will not hesitate to take their business elsewhere. Your message must spell out your goals and every mission. The best message for your brand takes the customer’s needs into account.

It is worth asking yourself whether your product or service will help people survive. According to psychologist Abraham Maslow, needs sit in a pyramid, and you should weigh your services or goods by how vital they are. Physical needs sit near the bottom and psychological ones higher up. The most basic needs we share as humans are food, sex, protection, and shelter. Above that sits the need for fellowship and companionship. The need for self-actualization tops the pyramid. Knowing this hierarchy gives you the leverage to sharpen your message and pull customers in. Acceptance is what we all crave. We want to feel like we belong somewhere. We all must eat and drink. Use what you have learned to show how your product helps consumers meet those needs and thrive. If you run technical enhancement training, for instance, your website must say clearly that you teach people. Then craft a message that ties technical training to your customers’ survival needs. Show how professional training lets them earn more money and take better care of themselves. Being skilled also helps them build friendships with respected members of society.

chapters.title: Clear messaging that speaks to customer needs is at the heart of effective marketing
chapters.text:
There is no room for uncertainty in a brand message. Aesthetics matter less than effective language. You may have the most beautiful website on the block, but if your language is unclear, that elegance is wasted. Your message should pass along three things: who you are, what you do, and why you are the best fit for the job. Customers should grasp your message and connect with your story without strain. If they keep running into friction, they will not hesitate to take their business elsewhere. Your message must spell out your goals and every mission. The best message for your brand takes the customer’s needs into account.

It is worth asking yourself whether your product or service will help people survive. According to psychologist Abraham Maslow, needs sit in a pyramid, and you should weigh your services or goods by how vital they are. Physical needs sit near the bottom and psychological ones higher up. The most basic needs we share as humans are food, sex, protection, and shelter. Above that sits the need for fellowship and companionship. The need for self-actualization tops the pyramid. Knowing this hierarchy gives you the leverage to sharpen your message and pull customers in. Acceptance is what we all crave. We want to feel like we belong somewhere. We all must eat and drink. chapters.title: A well-told story organizes information inside a seven-part framework
chapters.text:
Sometimes we get so caught up in a movie or novel that we lose track of time while enjoying the story it tells. A well-crafted story has the power to hold our attention and improve how well we remember it. The fact that a good story is organized knowledge is what sets it apart from ordinary speech. We enjoy listening to stories because of our need for order and consistency. Because it is structured, we recall stories long after they have been told. Storytelling is an effective marketing strategy that makes messages stick. Unlike the erratic honking of cars and odd noises we hear and forget almost instantly, a good story lingers in the mind after a single listen, much like a melody. Music follows a set of rules and patterns that are easy to recognize. A pattern can also be found in good tales.

Make a message as infectious as a melody by turning it into a story if you want to project your brand. The StoryBrand seven-part framework, also known as the SB7 framework, is a simple way to do this. The SB7 framework taps into the pull of stories. Its structure describes the seven most common story elements: a character, a problem, a guide, a plan, calls to action, a failure, and a success. The remaining chapters in this summary will walk through each of these pieces in turn. For now, here is a sketch of how the parts fit together.

In every story, the protagonist is the focus of attention. Their character sits at the center of the plot. They have a strong desire for something that seems out of reach. A problem causes this difficulty. Just as they are about to give up, a guide appears and proposes a solution to the dilemma. The guide instructs the character to behave in line with the plan. To escape failure, the character must stick to the plan and satisfy that first desire. This story arc can be crafted for every type of brand. Once you have sorted out your script, it will give you the firepower to win and keep the attention of your customers. The next chapter zooms in on the character.

chapters.title: In your business story the customers are the characters and you should focus on one desire
chapters.text:
Every good story has a hero. The Harry Potter series has Harry Potter. Game of Thrones has Jon Snow, depending on the season you watch. The hero of an effective brand story is the customer. Let your story revolve around your clients’ wants and needs. Tell the story from their perspective. It will stay with them, and they will automatically seek you out when they need something in the real world. The need will get tied to your brand.

Consider a travel company as an example of how vital it is to make the customer the main character. This company’s website features stunning scenery from around the world, their beautiful offices, and a story about themselves. In short, it covered everything but the consumer. This is a good example of what not to do. The message is fuzzy and does not answer the customer’s needs. They showed off their business rather than focusing on what their company will do for the consumer. People may enjoy the beautiful landscapes and offices, but they cannot connect their own needs to what is being said. Your website should talk more about the people who use it than about you. They should easily see why they need to contact you and what you bring to the table. In your brand story, the customer must be the main character. Engage them by focusing on their needs. Concentrate on one desire so it becomes more dominant. There is no point in mentioning all of your resources. It would just confuse the clients and make it impossible for them to see how your message addresses their needs.

Someone at the travel agency eventually realized that customers want to travel easily. As a result, the website was redesigned to emphasize how the services relieve customers of the pressure of making travel plans. The message became more succinct and direct, and everyone was clear on what the travel agency does.

chapters.title: Turning your customer’s internal problems into a villain keeps them interested in your offer
chapters.text:
How does your product or service solve customer problems? The second component of the SB7 framework focuses on identifying the problems your customers face and offering solutions to them. Companies tend to sell solutions to external problems, but people buy solutions to internal problems. People like being heard. Simply naming the issues your customers face will pique their interest in the solution you offer. State the issue clearly, showing that you understand where the shoe pinches. By communicating your awareness of your customers’ challenges, you show understanding.

In a story, having a hero is not enough. There has to be a bad guy somewhere. As a result, this obstacle or dilemma that the customers face must be portrayed as the story’s villain, who must be defeated. If your product is a time management app, it helps to portray distractions as a villain. Turn all that steals time into a mini villain, and these villains will become the main villains. Internal issues may often be more urgent than external ones. Feeling stressed that you do not have enough time to relax could be an internal issue that a time management tool can help with.

If a customer’s home needs painting, the fact that you are a painter would not persuade them to hire you over another painter. However, by making a villain out of the fact that they may own the ugliest house on the block, you can show them how to defeat that villain by hiring you. Tell the homeowner that you are one of the only painters who can use paint to restore the house’s elegance.

chapters.title: A careful mix of empathy and authority will steer your customers in the right direction
chapters.text:
In the Lord of the Rings movies, Frodo bears the terrible weight of the One Ring. Like every other story, the hero finds himself in a difficult situation and needs to find his way out of it. In this time of confusion, a guide appears to clear the path and provide support to the hero. Frodo had Gandalf the Great as his guide. The guide can take any form. It could be a teacher, a parent, a team coach, or, in Frodo’s case, a wizard. The primary role of the guide is to enlighten the hero on what can be done to achieve something greater than what they thought was possible. In your brand story, your organization is the guide. Its aim is to help customers overcome obstacles in their lives.

There are two key attributes you must have to present yourself as an effective guide: empathy and authority. Empathy establishes the foundation for a trustworthy relationship. It shows your customers that you are aware of their situation and can feel for them. Only if you build a relationship with your customers will they take your advice seriously. Empathy builds trust, while authority comes from being consistently competent. Being overbearing or condescending does not create authority. Authority is won by acting with honesty and following through on your promises. You must show that you are capable of carrying out your promises. Deliver. A steady demonstration of competence is the key to building authority.

At this point, all of your story’s major characters have been identified. You know who the hero, the villain, and the guide are. It is time to tell a gripping story.

chapters.title: Laying out a plan is the key to getting customers to commit to a purchase
chapters.text:
The fact that your customers trust you and your ability to deliver does not mean they will commit to a purchase. Buying a product or service is a different process altogether. You must come up with a workable plan that will seal their decision to buy. Consider a group of people who need to cross a stream to get to the other side but do not want to get muddy. As a guide, your job is to throw stones into the water for them to walk on so they can reach their destination without getting wet. Your plan is made up of crossing stones. A process plan and an agreement plan are two ways of laying out a good plan.

Show how to make a purchase or make the purchase completely risk free for your customers. The process plan is a diagram that shows the customers what to do. It instructs them on how to buy and use a product. Explaining the process reduces customer frustration and increases the chance of keeping them. If a customer wants a product, they may struggle to decide whether the product is a right fit. You can help by listing the steps they need to take to confirm they are making the right choice. These steps form the process plan.

The second method, an agreement plan, helps to remove the fear of purchase. For example, offering a money-back agreement or assuring customers that they will leave with products that meet their needs and standards will boost the chance of a sale.

chapters.title: Direct and transitional calls to action guide customers toward a purchase
chapters.text:
Encourage your clients to take action. Do not wait for anyone to notice you. Every day an average of three thousand commercials reach customers. As a result, if you want to be selected, you must stand out from the crowd. Make a direct call to action to nudge your customers in your direction. Make a statement that is both bold and straightforward. Provide several calls to action on your website. Use a variety of phrases and place them around the site. Direct calls to action include phrases like Click Here to Buy, Buy Now, and Register.

Another way to get consumers to make a decision is to use a transitional call to action. It differs from a straightforward call to action in that it aims to build a positive relationship with consumers rather than trying to persuade them to make a purchase. The goal remains to move them toward a purchase. Direct calls encourage customers to place an order, while transitional calls encourage them to return to you next time they have a similar need. Each time they encounter the problem your product solves, they think of you and not the competition. To do this, you need to offer them something of value free of charge. Kind gestures stick with customers and make them come back in the future.

chapters.title: Showing the implications of failure can motivate customers to buy
chapters.text:
The possibility of the hero getting killed is why we stay glued to the screen in a movie. We want it to end well for the hero, but we are not unaware of the chance of the hero suffering a loss or losing their life. Jon Snow died in Game of Thrones and was not brought back to life until the next season. That kept everyone on the edge of their seats.

Your brand story should lean on the fear of failure, because that fear guides our buying choices. Behavioral economist Daniel Kahneman published a paper in 1979 that showed the dissatisfaction people feel after a loss is usually greater than the satisfaction they feel from a gain, even when the amount is the same. For example, it will hurt more to lose five hundred dollars than to gain five hundred. When making buying decisions, people care more about avoiding loss than pursuing gain.

So you must be explicit about the drawbacks of not buying from you. For example, a professional advancement training organization that aims to teach people how to give public speeches must mention the risk of career stagnation that comes with not knowing how to speak in front of a community. You must also show the dangers of delaying such training before they believe they will need it. An insurance company that seeks to protect people against potential losses must highlight those losses in their ads and show how buying their insurance will guarantee protection.

chapters.title: Share a vision that shows your customers how your product will transform their lives
chapters.text:
The fact that things can go wrong for the hero is one of the things that makes stories so powerful. When you are the protagonist in a novel, you do not want anything to go wrong. After dangling the risks of not buying in front of the buyers’ eyes, your company must spell out the happy ending that your product provides. Beyond the product, create a vision that your customers can share with you. Nike, for instance, has a slogan that says Just Do It. They use it to remind their clients that they are not just selling shoes and sports apparel. They believe in living a life that is full of inspiration, zeal, and glory, and they welcome every customer into that conviction. Starbucks was delivering more value than just coffee; they were delivering a sense of sophistication and enthusiasm about life.

How can you create a vision that your customers will be willing to share? Three strategies can help: status, completeness, and self-acceptance. It is time to sell. When you make provisions for something like a premium package that offers extra services others do not, you will find that people aspire to that status. People are drawn to what they do not have and what sets them apart from others. Sell the idea of completeness. Customers must see the possibility of fulfillment through your product. They should get the impression that without your product they are incomplete. As a result, they should aspire to surpass all others in order to be united with what you have to give.

Self-acceptance and realizing your ability is the third approach. Let people feel at ease in their own skin. Help them accept themselves as they are. They will associate with you if the product speaks to ordinary people and tells them there is nothing wrong with them. When the movie’s good-guy nerd finally wins the beautiful girl’s heart, it gives all male nerds a sense of identity, lifts their status, and makes them feel whole. There is a happy ending to every story. Nike was founded on January 25, 1964.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
You can make your marketing efforts effective and rewarding. Use the StoryBrand seven-part framework to create and communicate a clear message that addresses the needs of your potential customers. The framework has seven modules that are essential to telling a riveting story: character, problem, guide, plan, calls to action, failure, and success.

The main character in your brand story must be your customers. Everything revolves around them. It is their problem you are trying to solve. The story must focus on them. Identify a specific desire and turn it into a problem that needs to be solved. This problem is the villain that the hero of the story must defeat. Present a troubling situation that the hero must find help to resolve.

Your company is the guide that provides wisdom and help to the hero in their hour of need. Show clearly that you understand their frustration and have the capacity to help them out of the mess. Lay out the steps that must be taken to defeat the villain or reassure your customers that they are in safe hands, and the only outcome is success as long as they follow your guidance.

Prod them to take action by asking them to buy your product (a direct call to action) or encouraging them to build a relationship with you (a transitional call to action) that will make them remember you whenever they have this need. You can do this by giving them a memorable gift. Explain the dangers of not taking decisive action now and show them how making the purchase makes them part of something greater than themselves. Sell a vision and not just a product. The sense of identity and belonging will create a lasting bond between your brand and your customers.

Try this: to show your customers that your product will help them reach their dreams, associate your brand with someone who is already successful. Take Red Bull as an example. They associate with successful athletes to show that Red Bull gives you wings. To whom or what can you associate your brand to feed your customers with inspiration?
